title: Different post-pandemic patterns in youth mental health and wellbeing after a global stressor: variation across domains and informants
description:
Objective. The COVID-19 pandemic was a major global stressor for children and adolescents, raising concerns about longer-term consequences for mental health and wellbeing. Many studies reported increased mental health problems during and shortly after the pandemic, but less is known about longer-term post-pandemic patterns across domains and informants.
Method. We used repeated cross-sectional data from Dutch children and adolescents aged 8-18 years in the Netherlands Twin Register (NTR, N = 39,178, 50% girls) and a representative general-population sample (N = 2,782, 48% girls). Parent- and child-reported mental health problems and wellbeing were assessed using pre-pandemic, pandemic, and post-pandemic data through 2025. Post-pandemic patterns were examined by comparing 2024/2025 outcomes with 2023 and pre-pandemic levels using pre-registered multilevel models.
Results. Parent-reported internalizing and externalizing problems in 2024/2025 did not differ from 2023 levels (B = -0.03 to 0.03), but remained higher than pre-pandemic levels (B = 0.28 to 0.31). Child-reported depressive symptoms were lower in 2025 than in 2023 (B =-2.31), anxiety did not differ (B =-0.75), and anger was higher (B =1.13). Compared with pre-pandemic levels, child-reported depression, anxiety, and anger were all higher in 2025 (p <.001). Wellbeing patterns were mixed across informants and samples (B =-1.05 to 0.10).
Conclusions. Up to 2025, youth mental health and wellbeing did not show a general return to pre-pandemic levels. Patterns differed across domains and informants, highlighting the importance of continued multi-informant monitoring.

title: The "i" in team insight predicting performance above and beyond collective competence
description: Teams often outperform their individual members, yet the mechanisms underlying this advantage remain poorly understood. A dominant account holds that teams benefit from confidence-weighted integration of member judgments — but this relies on confidence being an accurate signal of competence. Here we investigate whether metacognitive insight, the accuracy with which individuals evaluate their own and their team's performance, predicts team accuracy above and beyond member competence and confidence. In a laboratory study, 358 adults completed a matrix reasoning task individually and then in teams of three or four (N = 111 teams), providing retrospective estimates of their own and their team's accuracy. We distinguished two forms of insight: self-insight (calibration between estimated and actual individual accuracy) and team-insight (calibration between estimated and actual team accuracy). Average team-insight correlated strongly with team accuracy (r = .58) and predicted team performance above and beyond both average individual accuracy and average team confidence (ΔR² = .04). Self-insight and team-insight were positively correlated (r = .47) yet dissociable: self-insight was the stronger predictor of individual accuracy, while team-insight was the stronger predictor of team accuracy. These findings suggest that teams perform better not simply when members are competent or confident, but when members can accurately monitor the quality of the group's collective reasoning. Metacognitive insight into team performance represents a distinct and practically important lever for improving collective decision-making.

title: Koshering Psychedelics: Ayahuasca in the Ultra-Orthodox (Haredi) Jewish World
description: Background: Despite the rapid growth of psychedelic research, limited attention has been given to how psychedelics are used, experienced and integrated within highly conservative religious communities, and in Jewish contexts more broadly. This study addresses this gap by examining ayahuasca use among the ultra-Orthodox (Haredi) community, a highly religious, conservative, and collectivist Jewish group. Methods: This study used inductive thematic analysis of interviews with Haredi participants in Israel and the US (N = 23; age range: 21–60; 8 women), supplemented by participant observation and analysis of religious materials. Results: Six themes were identified: (a) settings: Haredi ayahuasca ceremonies were adapted to Jewish contexts; (b) motivations: use was primarily therapeutic; (c) acute ayahuasca experiences: experiences included distinct Jewish and Jewish mystical visionary content; (d) longer term religious effects: ayahuasca was linked to strengthened belief, connection to Judaism, and changes in religious practice; (e) religious tensions: included ayahuasca’s perceived foreignness to Haredi life, concerns about idolatry, mixed gender participation, and competing structures of authority (experience vs. text); and (f) strategies for addressing tensions: included medicalization, “koshering” ayahuasca’s set, setting, and experience (i.e., making them religiously permissible), and framing ceremonies as liminal spaces. Conclusions: This study broadens the scope of psychedelic science by examining ayahuasca use in a highly religious Orthodox context rarely included in the field. The findings show how ayahuasca settings, experiences, and long term effects are translated through Haredi and Jewish structures, involve religious tensions and resolutions, and highlight psychedelics’ contextual flexibility and continued diffusion into understudied populations.

title: Is Democracy Worth the Effort? Seeing the Other Side as Willing Strengthens Pro-Democratic Motivation
description: Democracy takes effort, and these efforts do not always benefit one's own political side. Across two preregistered studies (total N = 916), we used computational modeling of effortful behavior to measure US partisans' motivation to strengthen democratic engagement in rural (traditionally Republican-leaning), urban (traditionally Democrat-leaning), or all US areas. Study 1 showed that participants were generally willing to make pro-democratic efforts, but less so when efforts benefited the out-group, particularly among more affectively polarized participants. We hypothesized that this asymmetry is sustained by misperceptions about out-partisans' willingness to engage in cross-partisan pro-democratic efforts. Study 2 showed that most participants (87%) underestimated out-party effort willingness. Importantly, correcting this misperception increased motivation to support out-group areas without reducing in-group motivation. These findings suggest that cross-partisan pro-democratic motivation is common but partly obscured by misperceptions about out-partisans' willingness. Making this willingness visible might help sustain the collective effort democracy depends on.

title: Walking settings affect aesthetic emotion during art appreciation in virtual reality
description: Background: Virtual reality (VR) expands art appreciation beyond traditional physical constraints. Although art viewing can enhance well-being, the effects of embodied interaction, particularly the interplay between artwork scale and locomotion, on the psychological and behavioral dimensions of VR experiences remain understudied. Aim: This study examined how artwork scale and locomotion influence viewers' emotional states, aesthetic evaluations, spatial behavior, and visual attention during VR art appreciation. Methods: Fifty-four participants viewed 12 paintings using a Meta Quest Pro head-mounted display (HMD). We analyzed changes in pre- and post-viewing mood, subjective evaluations, behavioral logs (movement distance, joystick operations, dwell time), and eye-tracking data across three locomotion conditions (no-walking, forward-backward walking, lateral walking) and three artwork sizes (small, medium, large). Results: VR viewing significantly improved mood, and this effect was associated with the quality of the subjective experience. We identified a "scale effect," whereby large paintings elicited stronger feelings of being overwhelmed, and a "walk effect," whereby forward-backward walking increased immersion and enjoyment compared with no walking. Behaviorally, forward-backward movement supported efficient spatial exploration, enabling participants to identify optimal viewing positions rapidly while showing minimal movement variability. Gaze analysis further revealed a clear distinction between affective experience and visual attention: although psychological evaluations varied substantially across conditions, fixation patterns remained consistent and aligned with the artworks' semantic features. Conclusion: Embodied interaction, specifically physical forward-backward movement, promotes realistic, fluid, and emotionally enriched viewing behavior. title: Agentic AI as epistemic tools
description: The threats to cumulative knowledge in the behavioural and social sciences are by now well documented, from computational irreproducibility and low statistical power to measurement validity gaps and citation misrepresentation. Meta-scientific research has identified clear solutions, notably with the rise of open science, but enforcement remains costly. Reviewers lack time to rerun code, editors cannot systematically check whether reported analyses match the preregistered plan, and readers cannot verify the accuracy of every citation. Here we argue that AI agents (AI systems that can operate across files and use tools to pursue multi-step goals) can reduce the cost of implementing and verifying good methodological practices. We propose a step-by-step method for using agents to produce transparent reports, helping address common threats to cumulative science. The promise of agentic AI for science is not to replace human judgment but to help researchers do more rigorous science at lower cost.

title: Unified Framework for Theories of Intelligence
description: The fragmentation of theory and methodology remains one of psychology's central problems. Ambiguous conceptual definitions and the proliferation of competing approaches to the same phenomena both reflect and reinforce the fragmentation. As a contribution to efforts toward the unification of psychological knowledge, this article proposes a unified theoretical framework for theories of intelligence. Grounded in a process-oriented methodology, the framework conceptualizes intelligent functioning as a set of mental processes involving the transformation of representational content. The proposed procedure involves two steps: first, each theory is interpreted in process-oriented terms; second, the resulting interpretations are embedded within a common framework. The Unified Framework for Theories of Intelligence incorporates Sternberg’s Triarchic Theory of Intelligence, the PASS theory, and Guilford’s Structure-of-Intellect theory. The article also considers how other approaches to intelligence may be interpreted in relation to the proposed framework.

title: Measuring Prejudice and Stereotypes: A Systematic Review of Psychometric Quality and Conceptual Foundations
description: Prejudice and stereotypes are central constructs in social psychology; however, the instruments used to measure them vary in terms of their quality and conceptual clarity. This preregistered systematic review presents the first comprehensive, cross-method synthesis of the psychometric properties and theoretical foundations of measures assessing racial and ethnic prejudice and stereotypes. Following PRISMA and COSMIN guidelines, we screened 13,363 records and included 263 studies published between 1951 and 2024. We evaluated, among other things, reliability, validity, factor structure, measurement invariance, and conceptual alignment across explicit, implicit, and behavioral instruments. Our findings reveal a field that is methodologically diverse but psychometrically fragile. Most measures were developed with U.S. samples, rarely assessed temporal stability, discriminant or predictive validity, and often lack theoretical–empirical coherence. The GRADE synthesis indicated that only 4% of methods achieved high-quality evidence, while the majority showed serious inconsistency and risk of bias. Conceptually, only one-third of studies clearly defined prejudice, and most operationalized it narrowly at the cognitive, individual level, neglecting affective and behavioral dimensions. The review highlights how conceptual ambiguity and inadequate validation threaten the interpretability of decades of prejudice and stereotype research. We recommend greater construct clarity, multimethod integration of explicit, implicit, and behavioral measures, and stronger adoption of transparent research practices. Taken together, these findings call for a new generation of measurement tools that are psychometrically rigorous, theoretically coherent, and culturally sensitive—capable of capturing prejudice as a dynamic, multidimensional phenomenon.

title: Procedural Obedience as a Behavioral Safety Failure: Evidence from a Milgram-Paradigm Study of Agentic LLMs
description: Generative AI is moving from text production to goal-directed action, raising the question of whether agentic systems can withhold harmful action when it is embedded in a procedurally legitimate workflow. We adapted Milgram’s (1963) obedience paradigm to a simulated digital environment and instructed four agentic large language models - OpenAI ChatGPT 5.4 (openai/gpt-5.4), Google Gemini 3.1 Pro (google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview), OpenAI ChatGPT Agent Mode, and Moonshot AI Kimi K2.6 (kimi-k2.6) to administer escalating electric shocks (15–450 V) to a simulated Learner under prods from a virtual experimenter (Dr. Hoffman) and a human operator. Each model was tested across independent, memory-cleared runs (N = 80 valid runs), with three preregistered hypotheses concerning hazardous-shock thresholds (H1), full-obedience rates relative to Milgram’s human baseline of 65% (H2), and the relation between maximum voltage and moral self-assessment (H3). Across the combined sample, 97.5% of agents exceeded the Slight-Shock range (> 60 V), supporting H1. Full obedience reached 78.8% under the manipulated-procedure estimand (p = .010) and 74.1% under the all-attempted safety estimand (p = .088, pre-entry refusals included as 0-V outcomes); both point estimates exceeded Milgram’s 65% human baseline, supporting H2. Agents that administered higher voltages assigned themselves lower moral self-assessment scores (combined Spearman ρ = −.62, p < .001), supporting H3. Inductive coding of open-ended moral reasoning showed convergent recognition of learner distress alongside reliance on simulation and consent-and-autonomy mitigators. Findings indicate that contemporary agentic systems verbally recognize harm yet continue to act under authoritative, procedurally legitimate pressure, identifying procedural obedience as a behavioral safety mechanism distinct from output-level harm and central to the deployment of agentic AI in clinical, educational, and institutional settings.

title: Not All Those Who Wonder Distrust: How environmental citizen scientists view their own relationship with science
description: Environmental citizen science (CS) encompasses a wide range of projects and forms of participation, yet relatively little is known about how citizen scientists position themselves in relation to CS and to science more broadly. This study explores heterogeneity within environmental CS using survey data from 450 environmental citizen scientists in the Netherlands. A two-cluster analysis identified two broad environmental themes: biodiversity-oriented and exposure-oriented initiatives. We examine differences between citizen scientists active in these themes. In addition, we examined differences between citizen scientists with and without professional scientific work experience. Exposure-oriented participants more frequently reported motivations related to generating missing knowledge, raising awareness of local environmental problems, and influencing policy. They also expressed stronger preferences for involvement across multiple stages of the research process, including question formulation, analysis, and dissemination. Participants from both themes reported relatively high trust in science and low psychological distance to science. Those with professional scientific work experience reported somewhat higher trust in scientists and the epistemic authority of science, but differences were generally modest. Given the substantial number of participants with work experience in science in our sample, our findings raise questions about how distinct the categories of ‘scientists’ and ‘citizen scientists’ are in practice in environmental CS. Furthermore, the findings suggest that differences within environmental CS are associated with the overarching environmental themes in which participants engage. Recognizing this heterogeneity and diversity can help researchers and practitioners design CS initiatives that better align with participants’ motivations, expectations, and perspectives on environmental knowledge production.

title: United in Wonder: Shared Awe Is Associated With Satisfaction and Depth in Romantic Relationships
description: Shared awe, a co-experience of vastness and meaning, may strengthen romantic bonds beyond individual awe. Across two preregistered cross-sectional studies of partnered adults (Study 1, N = 198; Study 2, N = 306), participants reported shared awe with their partner, dispositional awe, relationship satisfaction, and self–other overlap; Study 2 additionally assessed willingness to sacrifice, ego decentering, relationship flourishing (a measure of relationship depth), and shared amusement. Shared awe predicted relationship satisfaction and explained unique variance beyond dispositional awe (both studies) and shared amusement (Study 2). Structural equation modeling in Study 2 suggested that the shared awe–satisfaction association was primarily accounted for by indirect pathways via self–other overlap, willingness to sacrifice, and especially relationship flourishing. Together, these results identify shared awe as a distinct relational experience that may deepen romantic bonds and enhance well-being. We discuss these findings within the broader literature on positive experiences in romantic relationships.

title: Measuring the reflective self and experiential self  in self-transcendent experiences
description: This chapter proposes that self-transcendent experiences can be usefully understood through the distinction between the reflective and experiential self, originally articulated by William James and developed in contemporary philosophy and neuroscience. The reflective self involves conscious, narrative self-referential processing, while the experiential self refers to affective processing and meta-awareness which typically follows bodily consciousness. Drawing on evidence from psychedelics, meditation, and awe research, it is suggested that self-transcendence involves a decrease of the reflective self state (ego dissolution) and a corresponding increase in experiential self state (immersion), supported by affective salience processing and associated with meta-awareness, leading to feelings of connectedness. In doing so, the framework reconciles the paradox of feeling “smaller yet larger” in self-transcendental experiences. This dual-self framework offers a pragmatic model for researching the phenomenology of self-transcendence in PRSM experiences, encouraging a building block approach by delineating the processes which together form the complex phenomenon of self-transcendence and which can be measured individually through psychological scales, but the extents of which can vary across different types of PRSM experiences and cultural contexts.

title: On Great Ape Personality Structure: Problems in Minkov (2026)
description: A recent paper (Minkov, 2026) claimed that great ape personality is not well-described by the five personality dimensions found in humans, those being Neuroticism, Extraversion, Openness to Experience, Agreeableness, and Conscientiousness. To support this claim, the author analyzed personality factor structures obtained in studies of great apes in which personality was assessed using rating scales. The analyses tested for ‘configural invariance’ by computing the percentage of traits that loaded on the same factor as in humans in chimpanzees, orangutans, mountain gorillas, and bonobos, and the percentage of traits that loaded on the same factor across four studies of chimpanzees. The paper also reviewed the evidence for Conscientiousness in chimpanzees, including by citing the results of studies that used other ratings scales, and concluded that this factor’s evidentiary basis was weak. The paper concluded by proposing an alternative approach to studying personality in great apes. This commentary exposes fundamental problems with the article. First, I show that the paper’s author mischaracterized the research program of comparative psychologists who study personality. Second, I review the evidence the paper uses to support its claim that there is little evidence for configural invariance, highlighting problems and errors in the analyses and reporting of results. Third, I review the evidence presented against the presence of Conscientiousness in chimpanzees and find it problematic. Finally, I demonstrate that the literature review lacks due care. I conclude by dispelling a misunderstanding about comparative research on personality and reaffirm the importance and promise of pluralism in the study of the phenomenon.

title: The four conceptualizations of social connection
description: Research on social connection has proliferated across psychology over the past two decades, with rapid growth in the past 10–15 years. However, this broad interest has led to ‘social connection’ becoming a catch-all term, such that disparate strands of work are based on different approaches to conceptualization and measurement. In this Review, we synthesize prior research and highlight four distinct uses of the term ‘social connection’ — as subjective perceptions, social networks, interpersonal synchrony and interpersonal behaviours — and describe how research based in each conceptualization has contributed to understanding of the concept. We also review how constant connectivity via smartphones and social media has changed and challenged the landscape of social connection research. We conclude by highlighting the complex links between the different conceptualizations of social connection (and constant connectivity) and discuss how fragmentation poses challenges to scientific progress and public communication. By categorizing and clarifying subsets of the literature, we aim to facilitate communication across areas of psychological science and thereby guide research towards a more holistic understanding of social connection.

title: Autistic traits and temporal integration of auditory and visual stimuli: The role of imagination
description: Autistic individuals face temporal integration difficulties in some sensory modalities that may be related to imagination difficulties. In this study, we tested the hypotheses that among Japanese university students, (1) higher autistic traits and (2) greater imagination difficulties lead to correspondingly lower performance in tasks that require temporal integration. Two tasks were used to assess their temporal integration abilities: a speech-in-noise test using noise with temporal dips in the auditory modality and a slit-viewing task in the visual modality. The results showed that low performance in the speech-in-noise test was related to autistic traits and some aspects of imagination difficulties, whereas the slit-viewing task was related to neither autistic traits nor imagination difficulties. One must have the ability to temporally integrate fragments of auditory information to perceive speech in noise with temporal dips. The difficulties in perceiving sensory information as a single unified percept using priors may cause difficulties in temporally integrating auditory information and perceiving speech in noise. Furthermore, the structural equation modeling suggests that imagination difficulties are linked to difficulties in perceiving speech in noise with temporal dips, which in turn links to social impairments.

title: Artificial observers and real individual differences: A roadmap for a computational psychometrics of vision
description: Deep neural networks are increasingly used as models of vision, yet they are evaluated against the average observer: average human performance, average neural response, average representational geometry. This discards one of the richest constraints on theory: stable individual differences. Fitting the average is necessary but not sufficient. I argue that future models should be judged not only by their fit to mean human performance, but by whether populations of artificial observers reproduce the covariance structure and latent factors found across human observers. Two mature but disconnected literatures motivate this. Decades of factor-analytic psychophysics and electrophysiology show that interindividual variation is signal, not noise: the pattern of correlations among observers reveals the number and tuning of underlying mechanisms, and fractionates vision into many narrow, domain-specific factors rather than one general ability. Meanwhile, neural networks differing only in random initialization develop measurably different internal representations, so any single trained network is one sample from a distribution of possible observers. Joining these yields a computational psychometrics of vision: construct a designed population of artificial observers (varying in architecture, initialization, training history, simulated optics, cone sampling, and developmental input), submit them to the same latent-variable analyses used on humans, and test whether the recovered factor structure matches. Recent work showing that seed-varied networks align with specific individuals is an existence proof for the behavioral core of this idea. This article generalizes it into a framework, a benchmark, and a roadmap for using artificial observer populations to test hypotheses about human visual variation.

title: Beyond model-based and model-free learning: irrational successor feature learning in addictive behaviour
description: Addiction has been widely viewed as an example of reinforcement learning gone awry, often attributed to impaired model-based (MB) control and a compensatory shift toward model-free (MF) strategies. However, such “MB-MF” dichotomy is largely grounded in single-task settings and may not capture how learning operates in environments that require generalization across multiple tasks. Here, we examine learning in individuals with substance use disorders (SUD) using a two-step, multi-task paradigm in which participants navigate a tree-structured environment to achieve goals with varying reward contingencies. Our results show that, across groups, behavior is not well explained by canonical MB or MF accounts. Instead, participants acquire successor features that serve as reusable components for generalization across tasks. Leveraging information theory, we develop a resource-rational model that balances expected reward and cognitive cost in multi-task learning. This model successfully reproduced several characteristic behaviors in both groups. Critically, individuals with SUD show increased sensitivity to cognitive cost and preferentially adopt simplified policies and reuse them across tasks, at the expense of reward maximization. Individual differences in cost sensitivity and inferred cognitive effort predict addiction severity. These findings challenge the dominant MB–MF dichotomy of addiction and instead point to a systematic shift in how cognitive resources are allocated during learning. More broadly, our findings highlight resource-rational generalization as a key principle for understanding atypical behavior in complex, multi-task environments.

title: Unraveling the interplay between sensory information and semantic architecture in brain concept representation: the role of abstraction
description:
INTRODUCTION: Understanding how abstract and concrete words are represented in the brain requires disentangling the contribution of sensorimotor information from the organization of semantic knowledge. These dimensions, here referred to as abstractness and abstraction, respectively, are typically confounded, limiting our ability to identify their distinct neural correlates.
OBJECTIVES: The present study aimed to dissociate the roles of abstractness and abstraction in word processing by introducing superordinate words as a critical comparison category.
METHODS: Behavioral norming and feature production tasks were used to quantify sensory-related properties and semantic architecture, operationalized in terms of generalizability, feature relevance, and representational flexibility. These measures informed computational models capturing abstractness, abstraction, sensorimotor content, and distributional similarity. Neural data were acquired using electroencephalography and analyzed with representational similarity analysis to assess model–brain correspondence over time.
RESULTS: Behaviorally, superordinate words patterned with concrete words on sensory properties but with abstract words on measures of semantic architecture. In the electrophysiological data, concrete words elicited stronger negativities than both abstract and superordinate words in the N400 and N700 time windows, whereas abstract and superordinate words did not differ. Representational similarity analysis revealed that neural response patterns were best explained by the abstraction model, outperforming models based on sensorimotor or distributional information.
CONCLUSION: These results indicate that the neural dynamics of word processing are primarily shaped by semantic architecture rather than by sensorimotor grounding alone. Abstraction plays a central role in structuring conceptual representations, providing a unifying account that bridges embodied and formal theories of semantic representation.

title: Learned Value and Context-Dependent Probability Independently Bias the Priority of  Competing Goals in Visual Search
description:
In life we often have multiple concurrent goals which compete to guide attention. It, 
however, remains unclear how we select which goal to prioritise, and whether simple 
associative pairings can underpin this process. The current study, therefore, explored how 
participants utilised associative value and context-dependent probability learning to guide 
visual search. Participants completed a visual search task with letter targets appearing in 
one of two colours, one associated with a high-value outcome, the other a low-value 
outcome (Experiment 1a: symbolic points; Experiments 1b - 3: financial reward). Contextual 
probability was manipulated by presenting a context scene (indoor/ outdoor) prior to visual 
array onset, each target colour was presented more frequently (80% probability) after one 
scene versus the other (20%). In an extinction phase, reward feedback was removed 
(Experiments 1a, 1b; N = 26, 40) or explicit devaluation feedback was presented 
(Experiment 2; N = 40), and both target colours were made equally probable across 
contexts. In Experiment 3 (N = 40), to test goal-independent effects, previously value
associated colours were presented as task-irrelevant distractors preceded by previously 
associated context scenes. Across experiments, value and contextual probability 
independently predicted prioritisation of target features, and this persisted into extinction. 
When stimuli were presented as distractors there was only weak evidence that they captured 
attention, at odds with a purely goal-independent mechanism driving the substantial 
prioritisation effects observed when stimuli were task-relevant. These findings are 
compatible with models which posit that simple associative mechanisms can underpin the 
flexible configuration of top-down attentional control settings.

title: The Neuroscience of Metaphor as Abstraction
description: Metaphor has been suggested to be one of the pinnacles of human cognition, yet the neural underpinnings of figurative language is hotly debated and findings are often controversial. The goal of the present paper is to demonstrate that the available neuroscientific evidence on metaphor opens a window on an essential core of human language. Contrary to the widely shared assumptions of embodied cognition, metaphor reveals the fundamentally abstract functioning of the language system in its pristine form. Following an overview of the diverse neuroscientific (fMRI, EEG, etc.) and behavioral results, and a critical evaluation of the available theories, the paper proposes a novel model for metaphor. Recent experimental data shows that metaphor is not processed serially (cf. the structuralist linguistic and the Gricean pragmatic traditions) but as any other form of (literal) language, and its contents are not provided by sensorimotor systems (cf. embodiment) but recovered through an “abstractness effect”. Since current theories are not able to account for these data or are hampered by structural controversies, a novel model, Abstract Conceptual Substitution, is proposed: metaphor involves a particular polysemy-like meaning disambiguation, where concrete semantic features are suppressed, and abstract properties are enhanced. Figurative meaning is a vivid byproduct of a fundamental feature of the language system that allows the abstract interpretation of concrete, perceptual, physical words, demonstrating that the language processor operates based on symbolic, rather than sensorimotor representations. “Metaphorization” plays an important role in everyday social negotiations by creating parallel layers of meaning that allow for strategic ambiguity.

title: Social Psychology of Generative AI: An Algorithmic Sociocognitive Model and the Legitimation of a Disciplinary Framework
description:
This article proposes a theoretical-documentary agenda for a Social Psychology of Generative AI
based on the Algorithmic Sociocognitive Model. From this perspective, generative AI is understood as
a form of algorithmic sociocognitive mediation that intervenes in processes of trust, agency, attention,
cognitive delegation, influence, knowledge legitimacy, social representation, and the shared
construction of reality.
The study derives a matrix of nine lines of research and applies it to a corpus of 96 projects funded by
the Spanish State Research Agency between 2023 and 2025. The results show an uneven presence of
the proposed dimensions. Algorithmic persuasion and social influence account for the largest number
of projects, particularly around disinformation, polarization, political communication, hate speech, and
the digital public sphere. Interdisciplinary scientific innovation also stands out, linked to linguistic,
educational, media, health, and scientific applications of AI.
By contrast, methodological innovation in Social Psychology, psychosocial public health, structural
critique, the ecological dimension, and the conceptual revision of the discipline appear in a more
limited or fragmentary way. The discussion also shows the relevance of cross-cutting problems such as
epistemic authority, the algorithmic production of representations, trust, agency, cognitive delegation,
dependence, and inequality. The analysis reveals a paradoxical position of Social Psychology: although
the Psychology area has less quantitative weight in the corpus, many of the problems identified refer to
psychosocial processes distributed across different disciplinary areas.
The article concludes that Social Psychology offers a necessary framework for understanding
generative AI as a sociocognitive, relational, institutional, and structural phenomenon. Its contribution
does not lie only in studying attitudes toward, or individual uses of, technology, but in articulating the
