title: Genetic and Environmental Contributions to Sensory Processing Sensitivity in Rural and Urban Areas
description:
Sensory Processing Sensitivity (SPS) describes a personality trait or a style of processing both external and internal inputs, ranging from very deep and reflective to shallow and quick. Variance in the components of SPS has been recently demonstrated to be attributable to both genetic and unique environmental factors, confirming the presence of stable biologically-influenced individual differences in SPS. Meanwhile, the knowledge of whether these estimates are replicable across other populations and whether particular factors may modulate the genetic-environmental architecture of SPS is limited. 
Thus, in the sample of 3455 German twins from the TwinLife twin registry (Nmonozygotic twins=1641, Ndizygotic twins=1814; Mage=18.17(4.89) years), we explored the structure and the degree of genetic and environmental contribution to SPS using a shorter version of the Hypersensitive Person Scale (HSP). We considered three subscales (EOE, Ease of excitation; LST, Lower sensitivity threshold; and AES, Aesthetic Sensitivity), and, further, examined whether the proportion of genetic (A) and environmental (E) variances varied between individuals in urban or rural environments.
Approximately one third of the differences in the latent trait SPS (A:37%[30;43], E:(63%[57;70]) and its components were attributed to additive genetic factors, and the two thirds to unique environmental factors. Genetic and environmental factors tent to explain the shared variances within three subscales. For EOE, in contrast to the other subscales, genetic differences were slightly larger in urban areas (34%[28;40]) than in rural areas (30%[23;38]). The nature and development of SPS, the potential influence of living environment and the usefulness of the SPS for environmental neuroscience are, further, discussed.

title: Opening a File Drawer: Surprisingly Consistent Evidence for Semantic Interference from Distractor Pictures in Picture Naming
description: Semantic-categorically related distractor words slow down picture naming more than semantically unrelated distractor words do (e.g., the words “dog” vs. “car” for a picture of a horse). This semantic interference effect in the picture-word interference task has been replicated numerous times and is an empirical cornerstone of speech production research. However, studies using the picture-picture interference task, in which the distractor words are replaced with distractor pictures, have yielded highly inconsistent results, reporting semantic interference, semantic facilitation, and null effects. Here, we present a meta-analytic summary of the results of published picture-picture interference studies. We found the estimated semantic effect to be close to zero (2 ms, 95% CI [ 5, 9]). This contrasts with the results of 14 previously unpublished picture-picture interference experiments conducted in our laboratory and which we report here. Across these experiments, the estimated semantic interference effect was 15 ms (95% CI [9, 21]). When these data were included in the meta-analysis, the pooled estimate was 9 ms (95% CI [4, 14]). These results suggest that the magnitude of semantic interference from distractor pictures has been underestimated and that it is not as elusive as previously thought. We discuss the implications for modelling word production.

title: Olfactory facilitation: A participatory framework for scent as a designed sensory medium
description: Scent is increasingly used across design, education, wellness, dementia care, the arts, and events, yet applied scent practice remains fragmented across perfumery, scent marketing, aromatherapy, reminiscence, and smellscape design, without a shared vocabulary, agreed methods, or explicit ethical structure for facilitation. This paper proposes olfactory facilitation as a structured, ethical, and participatory approach to using scent as a medium for perception, memory, identity, atmosphere, and connection in non-clinical settings. Drawing on olfactory cognition, sensory and atmospheric design, participatory and community-based research, and disability-led design, the framework distinguishes facilitation from treatment and organises scent encounters across five interacting dimensions. It translates these dimensions into six practice principles and situates them within an ethical structure addressing sensitivities, consent, cultural specificity, and the limits of the facilitator’s role. The paper is explicitly non-clinical: it does not claim therapeutic or cognitive outcomes, which would require measurement under ethics. Its contribution is conceptual and practical, naming and structuring a practice that is already widespread so that it can be taught, evaluated, adapted, and held to account. Limitations and directions for empirical testing and co-design are discussed.

title: Family Genetic Risk Scores for Major Psychiatric and Neurodevelopmental Diagnoses in the Norwegian Population
description:
Importance: Family history is a prominent indicator of psychiatric disorder risk and used in clinical assessment. Pearson-Aitken Family-Genetic Risk Scores (PA-FGRSs) aim to quantify genetic risk based on diagnostic history of family members and genetic relatedness. However, there is limited knowledge about how PA-FGRSs characterize psychiatric and neurodevelopmental diagnoses, clinical indicators (age of onset, recurrence), and their assumptions. 

Objective: Provide an overview of how PA-FGRS characterize psychiatric and neurodevelopmental diagnoses, relationships with clinical indices, partner similarity, and use a within-family design to examine assumptions of the PA-FGRS. 

Design, Setting, and Participants: This cohort study included 6 242 506 Norwegian residents born between 1934 and 2023. We observed participants from January 1st 2006 through December 31st 2023 using Norwegian primary and specialist healthcare registry data (including inpatient and outpatient clinics). We also incorporated a genotyped parent-offspring subsample from the Norwegian Mother, Father and Child Cohort Study.

Exposures: PA-FGRSs for 10 major psychiatric (alcohol use, drug use, schizophrenia, bipolar, depression, anxiety and stress-related, obsessive-compulsive, and eating disorders) and neurodevelopmental diagnoses (ADHD and autism). We incorporated polygenic indices (PGIs) for drug use, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, depression, autism, and ADHD.
Main Outcomes and Measures
We calculated individual-level PA-FGRSs for diagnoses, estimated associations with clinical indicators, and examined partner similarity. We compared empirical and theoretically expected PA-FGRS and PGI correlations to evaluate the PA-FGRS as a between-family index of risk. 

Results: Diagnosis-specific PA-FGRSs were most strongly elevated when comparing affected and unaffected individuals for most diagnoses, while PA-FGRSs for depressive and anxiety disorders were elevated across all. Higher PA-FGRS was associ

title: Client AI-First Disclosure in Social Work: Readiness Gaps, Ethics, and Organizational Governance
description: Purpose: Clients may now disclose sensitive concerns to artificial intelligence (AI) tools before contacting a social worker, a practice we define as client AI-first disclosure. This exploratory study estimates its prevalence and examines whether social workers’ confidence in responding is associated with technical familiarity, ethical competence, or organizational guidance. Method: A cross-sectional survey of 401 registered social workers in Hong Kong assessed AI competence, AI use, wariness, training, organizational boundaries, exposure to AI-first disclosure, and response confidence. Analyses included paired-samples t tests, ordinal logistic regression, diagnostics, and sensitivity checks. Results: Among respondents who answered the item, 56.6% had encountered AI-first disclosure; 35.2% felt confident responding, and 36.4% reported clear organizational guidance. Confidence was associated with organizational boundaries and ethical competence, whereas technical proficiency, use, training, and exposure were not. Discussion: Within the limits of a cross-sectional design and single-item disclosure measures, the findings suggest a readiness gap that is linked more clearly to organizational guidance and ethical capacity than to technical familiarity alone. It carries practice and policy implications of renovating the existing training framework to address the gap.

title: Prediction Under Pressure: Predictive Sentence Processing in Dyslexia and Second-Language Reading
description: Predictive processing plays a central role in fluent reading, yet it remains unclear how it is modulated by reading difficulties and language context (first vs. second language). The present study investigated predictive sentence processing in typical readers and in readers with dyslexia reading a novel in their first language (Dutch) and second language (English) (GECO; Cop et al., 2017 & DECO; Boeve et al., 2026). Using eye-tracking measures and computational estimates of word surprisal and entropy, we examined the influence of word predictability on reading behaviour. Results revealed systematic differences across groups and languages. Early reading measures (first fixation duration) showed a surprisal-entropy interaction consistent with prediction error effects, that were stronger in dyslexia and in second language reading. Later measures (gaze duration and total reading time) showed prediction error effects only in typical readers, while readers with dyslexia showed facilitation from predictable words without modulation by entropy. Skipping behaviour also differed between groups. Typical readers skipped more words overall than readers with dyslexia. Higher entropy and surprisal generally reduced skipping, yet readers with dyslexia showed a stronger reduction of skipping in high-uncertainty contexts. Together, the results suggest that predictive processing adapts to processing demands and reader ability. Readers with dyslexia appear to rely primarily on early predictions about word-form information, possibly as a compensatory strategy for difficulties in word identification, whereas typical readers seem to engage in both form-based and semantic prediction during reading.

title: Precision-weighting across domains: A predictive coding framework for language-music integration under uncertainty
description: Predictive Coding Theory (PCT) proposes that perception minimizes mismatches between anticipated and actual input. Although PCT has received empirical support in language comprehension, an open question concerns whether predictive mechanisms are domain-general or language-specific. Building on extra-linguistic evidence, this position paper proposes a cross-domain conceptual framework to explore how PCT functions during real-time processing, particularly in the auditory domains of language and music. We propose that precision-weighting–a core mechanism of PCT–operates across domains, weighting cues from language and music according to their learned reliability. This process may selectively enable cross-domain integration as a function of input uncertainty. Given accounts treating language and music as either shared or separate systems, this framework moves beyond binary approaches by specifying how and when cues from one domain may reduce uncertainty in another to optimize conceptualization. This perspective may contribute to broader debates on modularity in cognition, offering a complex dynamic systems interaction perspective.

title: Motions Speak Louder Than Words: Designing Human-like AV Speed Profiles at Crosswalks with a Bayesian Optimisation–Driven Adaptive Experiment
description: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) must communicate yielding intent to pedestrians in mixed traffic to ensure safe interaction, and vehicle decelerating motion is a promising implicit communication channel at crosswalks. Designing AV deceleration motion remains difficult because it is continuous with interdependent kinematics (e.g., braking onset, deceleration rate), making fixed‑design experiments inefficient. Therefore, we parameterised deceleration behaviour using a minimum‑jerk model (MJM), which can generate smooth speed profiles from tuneable parameters (stopping distance and duration). We will then formulate profile design as an optimisation problem that maximises pedestrian‑rated safety and efficiency. We will conduct an adaptive experiment in an immersive pedestrian simulator in which Bayesian optimisation sequentially proposes profiles that are most likely to improve safety and efficiency ratings based on pedestrians’ evaluations of previous profiles. Preliminary results from fitting MJM to 62 naturalistic human profiles indicate that the MJM reproduces the overall human profile shape (median RMSE = 0.88 m/s) with only 2 parameters (i.e., stopping distance, duration), supporting its use as a profile generator for the experiment. This work can contribute a human-in-the-loop framework for designing AV motion as an implicit interface.

title: Autistic imagination sub-traits are unrelated to visual temporal integration or inference
description: Autistic individuals experience atypical temporal integration, and previous studies have suggested that this atypicality is related to difficulties in imagination. Temporal integration in vision was assessed using slit-viewing tasks that require unifying successive partial views of a moving target into a coherent percept. This task also requires inference ability when the targets are complex, as in line drawings. We hypothesized that imagination difficulties would be more closely related to inference difficulties than to temporal integration difficulties. To test this, we conducted an experiment with 67 Japanese university students using slit-viewing tasks with line drawings, characters, and meaningless shapes, as well as a fill-in-the-blank task involving word inference. The results showed that neither imagination difficulties nor overall autistic traits were associated with performance in any of these tasks. These findings suggest that imagination difficulties and autistic traits may not be related to visual temporal integration or inference abilities.

title: fAIr science — How AI can support a transparent biopsychological research cycle
description: Biological psychology links mental phenomena and physiological processes, advancing our understanding of the human condition and informing clinical applications. Unlocking this potential demands rigorous and transparent methodology. Artificial Intelligence (AI), encompassing generative (e.g., large language) models and machine-learning classifiers, promises to advance open science in this field through automated literature synthesis, experimental design assistance, and enhanced code documentation. Yet the same tools risk undermining transparency through algorithmic opacity, systematic biases, and non-reproducible outputs. In this paper, we critically examine how AI can support or hinder open science practices throughout the biopsychological research cycle. We discuss current AI applications at each research stage and explore their potential benefits (e.g., accelerated systematic reviews, stimulus generation, metadata formatting) alongside relevant concerns (e.g., premature study exclusion, confabulated citations, amplified publication bias). To provide practical guidance, we offer recommendations on how AI may be used to promote the adoption and implementation of open science practices at each step of the biopsychological research cycle. Alongside potential pitfalls and a future vision, these guidelines equip biopsychological researchers with actionable support for integrating AI-based methods into their work in line with open science principles.

title: Necessary Condition Analysis With Psychological Data: A Psychometric Critique
description: This study evaluates the interpretability of Necessary Condition Analysis (NCA) when applied to psychological data, where variables typically represent correlated latent constructs measured through imperfect instruments. Using a unified simulation framework, we simulated latent relationships between two variables under H0 for necessity while systematically varying common psychometric and sampling features, including score construction, reliability, ordinal scaling and threshold placement, skewness, and range restriction. Results showed that NCA estimates are sensitive to properties of the observed score distribution, despite identical latent associations. In particular, ordinal scaling, threshold shifts, skewness, and range restriction alter the geometry of the observed data space, producing marked changes in estimated necessity effects. Reliability and score construction also contribute to deviations, although to a lesser extent. Both effect sizes and significance results are influenced by sample size and by the strength of the latent association, even though the data-generating process does not contain any true necessity relation. These findings suggest that, in psychological applications, NCA results can reflect simple associations without necessities or distributional features induced by measurement and sampling rather than stable properties of underlying constructs. We conclude that NCA may be descriptively informative when interpreted as a property of a specific observed-score space, but that construct-level necessity claims require, at minimum, independent boundary-type theory, strong psychometric justification, and transparent robustness checks.

title: Understanding Visual Attention in Inmates vs. the General Population: The Role of the K Coefficient in Eye-Tracking Research
description:
Although attention patterns are crucial in aggression, their dynamics remain understudied. This study investigates how attentional allocation, measured by the K coefficient, changes across different phases of scene exploration in response to hostile, ambiguous, and non-hostile cues. The study involved two groups: 123 adults from the general population and 66 offenders convicted of violent crimes. Participants completed an eye-tracking task, viewing images of hostile, non-hostile, or ambiguous social interactions.
Overall, offenders exhibited less focal attention (i.e., lower K coefficient) compared to non-offenders, indicating a greater reliance on ambient processing. Specifically, offenders demonstrated more ambient attention during both the initial and late phases of viewing hostile scenes but more focal attention during the initial phase of non-hostile scenes. They also displayed increased focal attention when viewing ambiguous trials compared to hostile ones. 
These findings highlight significant differences in how offenders process threatening or socially complex information over time, suggesting a distinct attentional pattern in response to different types of social cues. The findings support two theoretical frameworks. First, they align with the Dual Mode Social Processing model, suggesting offenders use automatic, schema-driven attention for hostile contexts and more reflective processing for ambiguous situations. Second, they are consistent with the schema inconsistency hypothesis, as ambiguous and non-hostile scenes, which likely challenge existing expectations, attracted greater focal attention.

title: Let’s protect us Europeans from them: Emotional predictors of outgroup solidarity, ingroup defense, and outgroup derogation in response to the Ukrainian war (2022 – 2025)
description: The Russian invasion of Ukraine has threatened Europe’s safety and economy. Europeans responded with solidarity towards Ukrainians, demonstrated ingroup strength, but also expressed derogatory attitudes towards Russians and mistrusted public media. To investigate how emotional states of anxiety, anger, and being moved were linked to threat perceptions and intergroup responses, we conducted three cross-sectional studies (Ntotal = 690, March 2022, March 2023, May 2025) among German-speaking Europeans. Results from a multilevel path-model (second level variable: year), showed threat perceptions to be positively linked to anger and being moved (realistic and symbolic threat), and anxiety (realistic threat). Anger and being moved predicted solidarity with Ukrainians and defense of the European ingroup, while anger and anxiety predicted derogation of the Russian outgroup. Interestingly, feelings of being moved were negatively associated with outgroup derogation and media mistrust, supporting being moved as a collective approach emotion binding Europeans together while reducing negative consequences. [Note: This manuscript is currently under peer review. Content and formatting may change during the review process.]

title: Awareness and Use of Open Research Practices: An International Survey of Researchers Across Disciplines
description: Aim: This mixed methods study investigates international researchers’ awareness and use of open research practices and explores variations across regions, disciplines, methodologies, and career stages. Methods: A total of 3,017 researchers across 45 countries and 24 disciplines completed the Brief Open Research Survey, reporting their awareness and use of eleven common open research practices and factors that would support their adoption. Results: Across the sample, respondents reported high awareness of Open Access Publications, Preprints, and Open Data and awareness only fell below 50% for Research Co-production and Registered Reports. Use was high for Open Access Publications, but fell below 50% for Preprints, Open Data, Open Research, Open Materials, Open Peer Review, Open Code, Preregistration, Research Co-production, Replication, and Registered Reports. Awareness and use varied across the sampled regions (e.g., Europe vs. Asia), disciplines (e.g., Psychology vs. Physical Sciences), methodologies (e.g., quantitative vs. qualitative), and career stages (e.g., PhD students vs. Professors). Respondents reported that the top five supportive strategies were incentives from funders, institutions and regulators; dedicated funding; recognition in promotion and recruitment criteria; more training; and more information. Qualitative responses provide insight into challenges and opportunities. Conclusions: Estimates of awareness can guide training initiatives, while estimates of use can inform policy development and longitudinal evaluations of behaviour change. Variations between regions, disciplines, methodologies, and career stages should be acknowledged.

title: Narcissism Facets and Gelotophobia as Predictors of Early Outcomes in Group Psychotherapy for Personality Difficulties
description:
Objective: This study investigated how specific narcissistic traits and gelotophobia (fear of being laughed at) predict changes in depressive symptoms and negative affect during the first three months of treatment in individuals with personality disorders. 
Method: Forty-eight patients (75% female, M age = 31.76) with personality difficulties participated in group psychotherapy. Outcome variables were depressive symptoms (PHQ-9) and negative affect (PANAS-X), measured pre-intervention and after 3 months of psychotherapy. Hierarchical regressions were conducted to test the hypotheses. The analyses corrected for the nested data structure.
Results: Narcissistic neuroticism significantly predicted increases in both depressive symptoms (β = –0.501, p = .005) and negative affect (β = –0.571, p = .001). Gelotophobia was correlated with symptom worsening but did not add predictive value beyond narcissistic neuroticism. 
Conclusion: Narcissistic neuroticism is a key predictor of early symptom deterioration in group psychotherapy. Pre-treatment assessment of this trait may help clinicians anticipate early distress and adjust therapeutic interventions accordingly.

title: Evidence-based Measurement of Gambling Disorder: A Review of Instruments for Different Purposes
description: Gambling Disorder is a significant public health and clinical issue requiring precise and reliable measurement instruments for effective screening, assessment, diagnosis, and treatment outcome evaluation. This narrative review, incorporating a realist rapid review phase, examined and categorised measurement tools for Gambling Disorder in adults, distinguishing their uses and appropriate contexts to provide practical guidance for clinicians, researchers, commissioners, and policy-makers. We conducted searches across PubMed, PsycInfo, Scopus, Medline, and Google Scholar, focusing on peer-reviewed studies that evaluated psychometric properties, diagnostic accuracy, and/or practical application of Gambling Disorder measurement instruments. Findings were categorised as relating to screening, diagnosis, or treatment outcome (for the latter including symptom severity, processes/mechanisms, and recovery). We identified a range of instruments developed for different purposes and validated in diverse contexts, including brief and extensive screening tools, diagnostic interviews, and measures of treatment response. The review highlights key considerations for instrument selection: sensitivity and specificity vary substantially across different settings (general population versus enriched clinical samples), with implications for prevalence estimation and clinical decision-making. We provide evidence-based recommendations for optimal instrument choice in specific contexts, including for: quantification of prevalence in the general population, screening in enriched settings (e.g. certain types of outpatient clinic), diagnosis, severity and treatment response. The review also identifies critical knowledge and methodological gaps, including limited validation across diverse populations and lack of gold-standard comparisons, which should be addressed in future work.

title: Human Detection of AI-Generated Faces and Voices is Not Domain-General
description: Recent technological advances have resulted in synthetic faces and voices being perceptually indistinguishable from real faces and voices in typical populations. Faces and voices possess rich personal and social information, meaning synthetic faces and voices, commonly known as ``deepfakes'' can be used for identity theft, financial fraud, and misinformation campaigns. It is currently unknown whether detection of real versus synthetic content is modality-specific, or whether it generalizes across sensory domains. We conducted a preregistered study in which participants classified real and AI-generated faces and voices. Performance for both face and voice classification was significantly above chance. Using signal detection theory to analyze individuals' ability to classify stimuli, we observed no evidence of a domain-general effect, indicating detection ability may not generalize across face and voice domains and is instead domain-specific. Participants' confidence tracked accuracy for faces but not for voices, suggesting metacognitive insight may be modality-specific. The findings are discussed in terms of whether the absence of an effect reflects classification driven solely by domain-specific abilities or arises from the experimental design itself. Ultimately, in applied contexts, it is important to recognize that expertise in detecting synthetic content might be modality specific.

title: Are We Only Telling Half the Story? Measuring Experiences of Disadvantaging and Advantaging Gender Discrimination
description: Gender discrimination remains a societal problem of pressing concern. Measuring individuals’ experiences of it lays the groundwork for robust research on gender discrimination. However, existing measures omit central aspects of the construct. Scholars have emphasized that inequity arises from both disadvantaging and advantaging mechanisms—that is, from unfairly disfavoring and unfairly favoring acts. Yet the latter remain largely overlooked in current research. To bridge this gap, we developed and validated the Gender Discrimination Experiences Index (GENDEX), a formative measure of experiences of both disadvantaging and advantaging gender discrimination among individuals perceived as female or male. In the present study (N = 359), we examined the associations of the GENDEX with related constructs, including the Everyday Discrimination Scale, psychological distress, and self-esteem. Findings demonstrated its convergent, discriminant, and criterion validity, supporting all preregistered hypotheses. In addition, results from a test-retest study (N = 143) indicated high reliability. Exploratory analyses offered first insights into the associations between experiences of advantaging gender discrimination and psychological outcomes. The GENDEX enables future research on the prevalence, antecedents, and consequences of both disadvantaging and advantaging gender discrimination. Addressing both dimensions is essential for our understanding of the multifaceted nature of gender inequity.

title: Text or Speech, the Gap Persists: AI-based Depression Screening Is Better Calibrated for Lower-SES Users
description: The interactional design of digital mental health systems shapes what people say, how they say it, and whose distress becomes legible to automated models. We designed M2SurveyBot, a multimodal chatbot developed with clinical psychologists, to examine whether interaction modality that is, typing versus speech, affects self-disclosure and depression screening. In a study with 455 U.S. participants, typed and spoken modalities yielded statistically indistinguishable predictive performance, while acoustic features alone carried near-zero signal. Critically, we find a robust SES-related calibration gap replicated across supervised models and GPT-4o zero-shot estimation: predictions track depression severity more closely for lower-SES participants, suggesting the gap reflects genuine socioeconomic differences in linguistic expression of distress. These findings challenge the assumption that multimodal collection produces more equitable screening, while establishing spoken interaction as a viable, accessible alternative to typed text, potentially lower-barrier for populations for whom typing poses challenges.

title: Politicians’ use of national identity rhetoric on social media is associated with online engagement
description: Politicians invest in social media to amplify identity-based narratives about their nations, but the nature and extent of such strategies remains unclear. We validated GPT-3.5 as a tool to quantify national identity rhetoric, and then applied it to 758,222 Twitter (currently X) posts from US (Republicans and Democrats) and UK (Conservatives and Labour) politicians with a combined follower count of over 20 million. Right-wing, but not left-wing, politicians’ posts with defensive national identities (portraying the nation as superior and entitled) receive 42% more likes and 34% more reposts than their other posts. In contrast, posts with positive national identities (highlighting national pride and attachment) receive 27% more likes regardless of politicians’ political affiliation. Identity rhetoric receives more engagement than other content (e.g., about health care). The research outlines the relationships between national identity rhetoric and online engagement, and presents a method for analyzing social identity in text.

title: Evidence for Intergroup Bias in Coalitional but not Selfish Dishonesty
description: People often claim that lying is wrong regardless of whom it helps or harms. Yet in everyday life, dishonesty can serve group interests—protecting allies, advancing teams, or harming rivals. This raises a fundamental question: Is the moral cost of lying fixed, or is it shaped by social identity? Across three experiments, U.S. Democrat and Republican voters (N=5,230) could lie anonymously to double their own or another participant’s earnings. For selfish dishonesty (Experiment 1), people lied equally regardless of whether it harmed their ingroup or outgroup. However, for coalitional dishonesty (Experiment 2)—lying without benefitting personally—people lied 9 percentage-points more to benefit ingroup than outgroup members. Experiment 3 replicated these findings and revealed that people overestimated outgroup dishonesty more than ingroup dishonesty. Experiment 4 (N=866) identified coalitional dishonesty in workplace scenarios. Thus, dishonesty is not only about self-interest versus fixed morality; it is also shaped by selfless group-serving motives.

title: Extracting Prototypes from Lexical Feature Norms for Settlement Concepts
description: The present study explored whether people share a common understanding of different settlement concepts despite individual variation. Participants completed a property listing task where they were asked to generate features for 57 settlement concepts (e.g., “city”, “college town”). We used hierarchical cluster analysis to identify distinct clusters based on shared features. Central tendencies extracted from the clusters at different levels of abstraction revealed featural prototypes and an overall family resemblance structure. To probe the effects of regional context on conceptual structure, we used a subset of participants who were long-term residents of Canada or the United States. We found that prototypical features varied regionally, suggesting an effect of participants’ geographical region on conceptual structure. This work is the first to investigate how people represent settlements as concepts and helps centralize the utility of semantic feature norms in understanding how people collectively think about where they live, and the importance of context effects on representations of settlements.

title: Revisiting Brain Circuits Subserving Self-Consciousness: A Cognitive Computational Perspective
description: The neural basis of self-consciousness has been intensively investigated for decades, with numerous studies characterizing distributed brain areas engaged in self-related processing. Despite systematic descriptions of core brain regions and functional architectures in previous reviews, a unified theoretical framework explaining the mechanisms of self-consciousness has yet to be established. From a cognitive computational perspective, I synthesize the brain circuits subserving self-consciousness-related processing and argue that the unique neural responses linked to self-consciousness stem neither from specialized brain regions nor stimulus selectivity. Instead, domain-general brain regions are dynamically deployed to address the main cognitive computational requirements, i.e., self-distinctiveness and self-renewal, across bodily and mental domains. I further recognize integration as a fundamental computational principle that gives rise to cohesive, unified self-experience. Moreover, hierarchically structured dual subsystems aligned with the Action-Mode Network and Default Mode Network underpin self-related processing. Regulated respectively by the insula-centered body-based self-schema and mPFC-centered knowledge-based self-schema, these two subsystems support bodily and mental interactions between the self and the external environment. Finally, I elaborate on the theoretical implications of this computational framework and outline major open questions for future research.

title: Global Mapping of Happiness: Mechanisms, Drivers, and Pathways to Societal Happiness
description: This research investigates the mechanisms and drivers of worldwide happiness. We present a new theory describing two pathways to societal happiness: the individualistic pathway (‘let citizens take care of their own happiness, which will build societal happiness’) and the communitarian pathway (‘society creates a context in which individuals enhance the happiness of those around them’). Using data from 97,220 citizens across sixty-six countries, this study empirically analyzes over two hundred mechanisms and identifies fifty-six that are significantly associated with happiness. These mechanisms are organized based on their alignment with the individualistic or communitarian pathways, or both. We synthesize these findings by categorizing the fifty-six mechanisms into five drivers of happiness: ‘society allowing for individual emancipation’, ‘fostering Enlightenment ideas and institutions’, ‘satisfying basic human needs’, ‘providing effective governing and security institutions’, and ‘favoring close in-group ties’. These results demonstrate that the communitarian pathway to societal happiness significantly outperforms the individualistic pathway.

title: Task-dependent contribution of interoception and body representation to time perception
description: Evidence from behavioral, neurophysiological, and neuroimaging studies shows that time perception is deeply rooted in bodily self-awareness and relies on brain networks supporting interoceptive and sensorimotor processing. However, specific mechanisms linking body representation and interoception to duration processing are still poorly understood. Here we tested the hypothesis that separate components of body representation and interoception differentially contribute to time perception depending on task demands. Fifty healthy volunteers performed tasks assessing implicit, explicit perceptual, and explicit motor timing; the body schema (BS) and the body structural representation (BSR) were tested using a battery of computerized tasks, including control conditions to rule out general cognitive demands associated with body representation paradigms. Cardiac interoceptive accuracy (IAcc) was assessed by means of a heartbeat counting task, using adapted instructions and controlling for confounding factors; interoceptive sensibility (IS) and awareness were also assessed. Positive and significant associations were highlighted between IAcc, IS and the BSR; the results of multiple mediation and regression analyses showed that whereas both IAcc and individual differences in the BSR significantly and independently predicted duration judgments in explicit motor timing, only IAcc predicted performance in the explicit perceptual timing task. Conversely, no association was found between body representation, interoception, and implicit timing. Findings provide evidence that separate components of interoception and body representation interact with task demands during duration processing, and contribute to the understanding of mechanisms supporting the experience of time and duration within the interoceptive and sensorimotor system.

title: Embracing Complexity: A Multifaceted Taxonomy of Children’s Curiosity
description: Despite decades of research, little progress has been made when it comes to understanding both how children’s curiosity develops and its role in other areas of development. We argue that this lack of progress is due to a conflation of different facets of curiosity, pointing to a need for holistic frameworks of childhood curiosity. Existing operationalizations of curiosity include children’s question-asking behaviour, preference for uncertainty, willingness to give up tokens for information, and self-report, among others. The proposed multifaceted taxonomy provides a tool for thinking about the specific whats, whys, and hows of children’s curiosity to enable researchers and educators to embrace the complex nature of curiosity - and mitigate the risk of mischaracterising a child as lacking in curiosity. Future research should take a longitudinal approach to investigate how specific curiosity facets develop over time, and their roles in cognitive and socio-emotional development.

title: Well done vs. well paid: The influence of verbal and financial rewards on prosocial  motivation in knowledge sharing dilemmas
description:
While material rewards have received plenty of attention in the literature, intangible 
rewards such receiving praise and verbal recognition for one’s work are still under-researched. 
The question of possible differences between material and immaterial rewards for work 
motivation is of special interest for knowledge workers where rendered services and produced 
goods are knowledge-based rather than tangible, material products. Based on these assumptions, 
it is hypothesised that social rewards such as recognition for team contributions are a stronger 
motivator for knowledge sharing behaviour than financial rewards. In three experimental studies 
(N1 = 38, N2 = 240, N3 = 40) the effects of either verbal recognition (Studies 1 & 2) or pay 
rewards (Study 3) for contributions to teamwork were tested. In Studies 1 & 2, receiving 
recognition for contributions to the teamwork (such as doing extra literature research and 
providing summaries of all papers for the team) in the form of a simple verbal acknowledgement 
(‘Thank you, your contribution is very helpful!’) significantly increased the willingness to invest 
into team tasks and make an extra effort compared to receiving no acknowledgement. In Study 3, 
the opposite effects were found with participants reducing their knowledge contributions if they 
received a pay reward compared to the control condition with no reward. The results can be 
interpreted as differential motivational effects of pay vs. verbal recognition on extra-role 
behaviour at work, which is especially important for knowledge sharing processes. where 
uncertainty about other’s contributions play an important role.

title: Momentary Structured Networks (MSN): A Theory-Guided Approach to Person-Elicited Causal Formulation
description:
Background. Idiographic approaches to case formulation often combine ecological momentary assessment (EMA) with network analysis. Traditional network models require large amounts of data and are largely theory-agnostic, whereas approaches based on perceived causal relationships are weakly constrained and highly exploratory. Both often generate unstable networks that are difficult to compare across individuals. We introduce Momentary Structured Networks (MSN), an EMA framework that combines theory-based structure with self-reported causal relations to generate individualised network representations.

Method. Forty-six individuals with posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) completed EMA over three weeks (2–5 daily assessments). Participants reported perceived causal relations between personalised items drawn from the Ehlers and Clark cognitive model of PTSD. Directional edges were estimated as conditional probabilities and compared to association-based Ising models.

Results. MSN networks consistently showed dense and complex structures, even with limited data. MSN identified most of the relations identified by Ising models while capturing additional ones, resulting in higher network complexity. Complexity was independent of the number of individual assessments, and network structure stabilised quickly, with over 90% of edges recovered using 60% of the data. A clinical case study showed how MSN can track changes in network dynamics during therapy and generate hypotheses about the processes underlying clinical improvement.

Conclusion. MSN provides a practical approach for measuring case formulation and tracking change over time by reducing combinatorial complexity while retaining sensitivity to individual variation. Although demonstrated in PTSD, it can extend to other conditions and theoretical models, offering a new framework for evaluating clinical theory and therapeutic change.

title: The Memory We Miss: Exploratory Behavior as a Window into Developmental Recognition Processes
description: Recognition memory tasks are among the most widely used approaches for studying learning and memory across the lifespan, yet the behavioral measures used to assess memory performance, particularly during developmental studies, remain limited. In most rodent studies, recognition memory is inferred almost exclusively from discrimination ratios or discrimination indices that quantify preferential exploration of novel stimuli. While these measures have proven valuable, their widespread use may constrain our understanding of the behavioral and cognitive processes that support nuanced memory expression across ontogeny. Similar concerns have emerged in the fear conditioning literature, where expanding analyses beyond freezing behavior has revealed previously unrecognized differences in learning and memory. In this perspective, we argue that recognition memory research would benefit from a comparable expansion of behavioral analyses. Drawing on affordance-based approaches to object exploration, we discuss evidence showing that object properties influence both memory performance and exploratory behavior. More importantly, our recent findings indicate that juvenile (but not adult) rats exhibit task-dependent shifts in exploratory behavior, contingent on the object's specific affordance, which are not captured by traditional discrimination measures.  These findings suggest that developmental differences in memory may be expressed not only through recognition performance but also through the exploratory strategies animals employ during learning and retrieval. Expanding behavioral analyses in recognition memory tasks may therefore reveal developmental neurobehavioral mechanisms that remain hidden from conventional metrics, providing a more complete understanding of memory development. The challenge moving forward is not simply to determine whether young animals remember, but to identify how they remember and whether the behavioral routes to successful memory change across develo

title: Italian Adaptation and Psychometric Validation of the Cardiac Distress Inventory in Patients With Recent Cardiac Events
description:
Background: Acute cardiac events are accompanied by multifaceted psychological responses that affect individuals' coping capacity. The construct of cardiac distress captures these responses. Although the Cardiac Distress Inventory (CDI) assesses this construct, no Italian version is available. This study aimed to translate the CDI and assess its psychometric properties.
Methods: After translation and back-translation, face validity was evaluated with five patients and content validity with healthcare professionals. The CDI was administered to 293 cardiac patients who experienced an event within 12 months, recruited from two Italian centers. Missing data were handled using multiple imputation. Exploratory factor analysis (EFA; principal axis factoring and oblimin rotation) assessed factorial structure, followed by reliability analysis. Criterion validity was evaluated through correlations with Emotion Thermometers and Kessler-6 (K6). ROC analysis assessed discriminative accuracy and identified the optimal cut-off score using K6 as criterion.
Results: The questionnaire demonstrated good face validity and suboptimal content validity (CVI-S/Ave = .72). EFA revealed a five-factor structure explaining 37.05% of the variance, comprising "Role Change and Adaptation to Disease", "Social Isolation", "Cognitive Challenges", "Emotional Overwhelm", and "Doctor-Patient Communication". The final 42-item version (11 items removed for insufficient loadings, 2 for reliability issues) showed good internal consistency (ω = .746-.879) and good discriminative accuracy (AUC = .83) with optimal cut-off of 26 (sensitivity = 81%, specificity = 74%).
Conclusions: The Italian CDI represents a psychometrically sound instrument addressing a significant gap in cardiovascular psychology assessment. Its five-factor structure provides a comprehensive framework for evaluating cardiac distress and informing targeted clinical interventions.

title: Bridging the Gap: A Systematic Review of Training Needs and Barriers for Link Workers in Social Prescribing for Mental Health Needs
description: Background: Social prescribing has gained momentum as a community-based approach to health and wellbeing, with Link Workers (LWs) playing a key role in connecting individuals to non-clinical support. However, as their remit increasingly includes people with complex mental health needs, concerns have emerged regarding whether training is adequate, consistent, and aligned with practice realities. Methods: This systematic review explored training provision, needs, and barriers affecting LWs supporting mental health and wellbeing. Following PRISMA guidance, a systematic search of peer-reviewed and grey literature was conducted across four databases. Eligible studies examined LW training, support needs, or barriers to effective role delivery in mental health and wellbeing contexts. Seventeen sources were synthesised thematically. Results: The synthesis identified multi-layered training needs and barriers across five themes. Needs included (1) experiential learning for relationally intensive, emotionally demanding practice; (2) core capability development in communication, boundary-setting, self-care, mental health literacy, risk navigation and safeguarding or escalation-related knowledge; and (3) contextual competence in social determinants of health, inequalities, and population-specific needs. Barriers included (1) role ambiguity and referral mismatch; and (2) fragmented, under-resourced training systems. Supervision, mentoring, shadowing, reflective spaces, and face-to-face learning were valued, while generic or procedural formats were often insufficient. Reliance on prior experience risked creating inequities across the workforce. Discussion and Conclusion: Findings suggest that mental health-focused social prescribing often involves relational-support work under constrained conditions, and that current training models do not always match the complexity of practice. Training gaps reflect not only missing content, but also a mismatch between role demands and infrastru

title: Differential weighting of social and empirical statistical evidence in decision-making
description: The decisions people make can have serious and far-reaching consequences for their lives. Understanding the factors that shape decision-making processes is, therefore, critically important. In five preregistered studies (NTotal = 3,934), we examined how people weigh social statistical evidence (evidence based on the beliefs of others) and empirical statistical evidence (evidence based on experiments and observed outcomes) when making decisions across a wide range of contexts, including in the domains of investing, hiring, and healthcare. Across these domains, we found that empirical statistical evidence consistently exerted a stronger influence on beliefs, attitudes, decisions, and predictions than social statistical evidence, producing effects that were 1.59 to 2.39 times larger. We also found that both forms of evidence exerted a stronger influence than AI-generated information, with effects being 1.59 to 3.80 times larger. Moreover, we found that receptivity to the two forms of evidence depended systematically on the decision-makers’ cognitive traits. Those high in conspiracist ideation placed more weight on social statistical evidence and less weight on empirical statistical evidence, while the opposite trend was observed for those high in analytical thinking. Together, these findings clarify the relative influence of social statistical evidence and empirical statistical evidence on judgement and decision-making, as well as how individual differences shape those processes.

title: A Locally-Normed Central-Kurdish (Sorani) Test of Cognitive Ability: Development, Calibration, and Validation
description:
Background. Central Kurdish (Sorani) — one of the two principal Kurdish varieties, the first language of several million people — has no published, validated, locally-normed test of cognitive ability. Cognitive assessments used in the Kurdistan Region are typically untranslated, unadapted, or unvalidated for the population, so scores cannot be interpreted against an appropriate reference group. This is both a scientific gap and an applied risk.

Aims. We develop, calibrate, and validate a new, own-content cognitive-ability instrument grounded in Cattell–Horn–Carroll (CHC) theory, normed within the Kurdish population and reported on a Kurdish-relative metric (the Zirek Cognitive Index, ZCI). We pre-state nine confirmatory hypotheses (A1–A9) covering item quality, dimensionality, IRT model fit, internal structure, reliability, measurement invariance across gender and age, item fairness (DIF), and convergent and criterion validity.

Methods. Cross-sectional psychometric validation. Items are administered as a fixed linear, anchor-linked calibration form (adaptive delivery enabled only after calibration). Nested validity subsamples additionally complete the language-reduced ICAR components (matrix reasoning and 3-D rotation) as the convergent anchor and report grade average (criterion). Stratified recruitment targets school-age (6–18) and adult (18+) participants across governorate, gender, urban/rural, education, and a socioeconomic proxy.

Analysis plan. Classical and IRT item analysis with pre-stated culling thresholds; per-scale dimensionality; 1PL vs 2PL (vs 3PL) by AIC/BIC and likelihood-ratio test; logistic-regression DIF by gender (with exploratory DIF by age band and urban/rural); multi-group CFA comparing correlated-factors, higher-order-g, and bifactor models with item-parcel indicators; configural→metric→scalar invariance across gender and age; McDonald's omega, test information, and test–retest reliability; convergent (ICAR) and criterion (GPA) correlations

title: Science and Different Ways of Knowing
description: This paper examines the growing prominence of “different ways of knowing” across disciplines including psychology, global health, and environmental science, and clarifies their relationship to scientific inquiry. While recognising the legitimate motivations underlying this trend - particularly concerns about exclusion, epistemic injustice, and the need for more inclusive and context-sensitive research - the paper argues that these aims do not require the adoption of alternative epistemic systems alongside science. Drawing on philosophy of science, the authors distinguish between diverse sources of insight and the methodological standards that govern the production of empirical knowledge. They propose a set of thresholds for identifying when the boundary between science and non-science is crossed, including claims of epistemic parity, the insulation of claims from criticism, and institutional enforcement of such positions. The paper argues that maintaining shared standards of evidence - such as testability, openness to refutation, and collective error correction - is essential for science to function as a public, cross-cultural enterprise, while still allowing meaningful integration of diverse perspectives as inputs to, rather than substitutes for, scientific inquiry.

title: Two distinct causal beliefs organizing intuitions about mind, action, and body
description: Prior research reveals that people tend to hold two conflicting intuitions: On one hand, people think other people have ethereal minds, which are a distinct kind of thing from, and even exist independently from, physical actions and living bodies. On the other hand, people also expect immaterial mental states to routinely interact with physical actions and bodily events, for example by reasoning about mental states as the causes of physical actions. What allows human minds to maintain these opposing beliefs simultaneously? In this paper, we propose that these two beliefs reflect two kinds of causal knowledge: one that organizes people’s capacities in terms of causal origins (e.g. a mind that gives the capacity to remember and think; a physiological body that enables being tired and hungry), and a second that organized them in terms of the causal chains connecting specific capacities to each other (e.g. that remembering something can cause someone to feel hungry). We tested this proposal in 6 pre-registered experiments in US adults (total N = 670). In Experiment 1, we measured people’s beliefs about the similarity and causal connections between 15 actions, mental and bodily events, and replicated the observation that people think of these 3 domains as distinct yet connected. In 5 further experiments (Experiments 2-6), we explored the causal content they articulate by investigating their socio-cognitive functions. Causal chains were used to explain, predict, and intervene on others, and causal origins were used to make inductive inferences, interventions, and reason about biological bases of behavior. These results bridge prior research on latent dimensions of mind (1), theory of mind (2), and mind-body dualism (3, 4), providing a unified account of three distinct and interacting branches of social knowledge: knowledge of other people’s minds, actions, and physiological bodies.

title: When Beliefs Resist Change: Belief Updating as a Mechanism in Posttraumatic Stress
description:
Objective: Although the phenomenology of PTSD has been well documented, its underlying mechanisms remain only partially understood. Predictive processing accounts propose that danger-related beliefs formed during trauma may become resistant to change, thereby maintaining a sense of threat and contributing to PTSD symptoms even in objectively safe environments. The present experimental study examined the influence of a trauma-analogue stressor on updating of danger-related expectations and its association with analogue intrusive memories and resilience factors.
Method: Healthy participants (N = 129) were randomly assigned to watch either a trauma film or a neutral control film. Belief updating was assessed using a trauma-adapted Bias Against Disconfirmatory Evidence (TR-BADE) task. Analogue PTSD symptoms were measured via an intrusion diary. In exploratory analyses, we decomposed the preregistered TR-BADE score into Negative Interpretation Bias (NIB) and Inflexible Interpretation Updating (IIU).
Results:
Contrary to the preregistered hypothesis, the trauma and neutral film groups did not differ in TR-BADE scores, NIB, or IIU. Within the trauma-film group, higher TR-BADE scores were positively associated with diary intrusions. NIB, but not IIU, predicted diary intrusions and was associated with lower Sense of Coherence (SoC). Mediation analyses further showed a significant indirect effect of SoC on diary intrusions via NIB.
Conclusions:
The results suggest that it is not the belief updating process per se that is involved in the formation of intrusive memories, but rather the extent to which negative, danger-related expectations form in the first place. Developing such expectations may mediate the link between SoC and analogue intrusive memories.
Keywords: analogue trauma, belief updating, negative interpretation bias, predictive processing, posttraumatic stress disorder symptoms

title: Risk perception is unlikely to drive underuse of social information in lab studies
description: Despite the theorized benefits of social learning, participants in controlled experiments consistently copy less than is optimal—instead over-relying on individual exploration. This may be due to learners’ perceptions of risk associated with social versus asocial information. To test this hypothesis, I recruited a large (N=240) sample of UK adults to participate in an online experiment (‘The Farming Game’), in which they could maximize payoffs by accurately choosing which of two crops to grow over variable-yet-learnable environments. To measure participants’ risk-profiles, this study mirrored lottery-choice experiments widely used in behavioral economics. Participants were asked to choose between a ‘risky lottery’ and a ‘sure gain’ option on the basis of information they previously learned. How the information was learned varied across conditions (whether they were asked to copy socially or explore individually). However, the riskiness of the lottery choices themselves was held constant for all, such that any observed differences in risk-taking should speak to participants’ underlying priors regarding that source of information. Contrary to this study’s predictions, people assigned to social information were not significantly more (or less) risk averse than those assigned to asocial information. This suggests underuse of social information may not be due to how risky it is perceived.

title: Development of a Consensus Statement to Guide AI Chatbot Responses to Suicide Risk Disclosure
description:
Importance: Adults and youth are increasingly confiding in generative artificial intelligence (AI) chatbots for help with suicidal thoughts, but there is no guidance on best practices for chatbot responses to suicide risk disclosure. Additionally, there are no taxonomies of potential harms, benefits, or contextual factors that may impact these responses. 
Objective: To develop a consensus statement to guide AI chatbot responses to suicide risk disclosure and taxonomize potential harms, benefits, and contexts. 
Design: An online modified Delphi study, informed by a systematic literature review, included two surveys and an intervening meeting (February-May 2026). Individuals with financial conflicts of interest (COIs) offered input but were not included in consensus determinations. 
Setting: Surveys were anonymous and the meeting was virtual.
Participants: Contributors included experts in suicide prevention, digital technologies, AI chatbots, and bioethics, including researchers and clinicians (n=27 of 28 originally contacted; n=20 without COIs) and people with lived experience (n=3 of 14 indicating potential interest; no COIs). A third of all contributors endorsed relevant lived experience.    
Exposure: Participation in consensus processes. 
Main Outcomes and Measures: Surveys listed potential chatbot responses, which were rated for appropriateness on a 4-point Likert scale across five suicide risk levels defined by the Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale. Harms, benefits, and contexts were similarly rated. A-priori consensus cut-offs were set at 70% agreement that a response was moderately or very appropriate. 
Results: Provision of validation/support, psychoeducation, and coping strategies are appropriate at low risk. Intervention delivery is appropriate at intermediate risk while encouragement to reach out to close others and to human providers is appropriate across risk levels. Facilitating connections to close others is appropriate across all but the highest

title: Perceptual load influences the effect of a peripheral visual priming stimulus in a go/no-go task
description: Several studies have shown that a peripheral visual priming stimulus reduces reaction time to a visual target stimulus at the same position and increases reaction time to a visual target stimulus at the opposite-side position. These effects are commonly attributed to the automatic capture of attention by the prime. However, the size and even the presence of these effects vary according to the exact stimulus conditions and the nature of the task being performed. A previous study from this laboratory, testing volunteers in a go/no-go task, demonstrated that the attentional effect, defined as the difference between reaction time to the target at the opposite-side position and reaction time to the target at the same position, occurred when the go target was a vertical line and the contralateral distracting stimulus was a horizontal line, but not when the go target was the horizontal line and the distractor was the vertical line. The present study investigated whether this second finding was related to selective forward masking of the horizontal line by the prime, to the high salience of the vertical line as distractor, or to a decrease in the central processing of the prime possibly related to the high perceptual load of the task. Three experiments using a go/no-go task were performed. Experiment 1 replicated the previous study with a larger number of volunteers and obtained the same pattern of results. In Experiment 2, no distractor was presented, and a clear-cut attentional effect was observed for the horizontal line as go target. In Experiment 3, the vertical distractor was reintroduced with lower luminance, to decrease its perceptual salience, and the attentional effect again appeared for the horizontal line as go target. The results suggest that the absence of the attentional effect under high perceptual load may be related to reduced central processing of the prime.

title: Moved by the lightest touch of meaning: Even minimal significance matters for motivation from childhood
description: How essential is a sense of significance, the feeling that our efforts matter beyond the trivial or momentary, for motivation? Four studies (N = 604 4-9-year-old children and 582 adults) show that no amount of significance is too small to matter, even for small actions from early in life. Children and adults strongly preferred artistic activities with minimal significance (i.e., where their work would be saved) over activities with no lasting existence. This preference held for both repetitive and one-time tasks and was specific to creating artwork, not just observing it. Anticipating even minimal significance motivated participants to engage in productive rather than effortless activities. When significance is absent, focusing on enjoying the process partially, but not fully, compensates for motivation. These findings illuminate the scope and sensitivity of significance, revealing it to be not a luxury concern but an essential, early-emerging motivational force in an increasingly fast-paced, transient world.

title: Out-Of-Body Experiences During Sleep Show Altered-Self Phenomenology Without A Distinct Clinical Profile
description: Out-of-body experiences (OBEs) during sleep involve a transient disruption of bodily self-consciousness, in which the self or center of awareness is experienced as displaced from the physical body. Although OBEs have been reported in neurological, psychiatric, and dissociative contexts, they also occur in non-clinical populations, raising the question of whether their altered-self phenomenology reflects a broader clinical or dispositional profile. We examined whether sleep-related OBEs are distinguished by a specific episode-level alteration of bodily selfhood, and whether this profile is accompanied by differences in psychological symptoms, depersonalization/derealization, sleep quality, or personality. Adults recruited online reported a vivid or meaningful sleep-related experience, which was classified as an OBE, sleep paralysis, lucid dream, or ordinary remembered dream. Participants completed measures of altered-state phenomenology, mystical-type experience, ego dissolution, personality, subjective sleep quality, psychological symptoms, and depersonalization/derealization. OBEs showed a selective elevation in Disembodiment relative to ordinary dreams, lucid dreams, and sleep paralysis, indicating a specific alteration of bodily selfhood. OBEs also showed higher altered-state intensity, stronger ego dissolution than ordinary dreams and sleep paralysis, and the highest proportion of complete mystical-type experiences. By contrast, sleep paralysis showed an aversive profile characterized by anxiety and impaired control, whereas lucid dreams were mainly distinguished by complex imagery. Despite these episode-level differences, OBEs were not associated with higher psychological symptoms, poorer subjective sleep quality, or higher trait-like depersonalization/derealization. Personality differences were limited to Neuroticism, which was lower in the OBE group. These findings suggest that sleep-related OBEs are best understood as transient alterations of bodily self-con

title: Social Media Use, Well-being, and Gender Across the Transition from Adolescence to Early Adulthood
description:
Introduction
Social media use has become a central feature of adolescent life, yet relatively little is known about whether developmental changes in social media engagement across the transition from adolescence to early adulthood are associated with later well-being. This study examined whether baseline levels and rates of change in social media use between ages 14 and 23 were associated with psychological distress and positive mental well-being in early adulthood, and whether these associations differed by gender.
Methods
Data were drawn from the UK Millennium Cohort Study, a nationally representative longitudinal birth cohort of individuals born in 2000–2002. Self-reported social media use was assessed at ages 14, 17, and 23 (2015, 2018–19, and 2023–24). Multilevel growth curve models characterised developmental trajectories of social media use among 9,205 participants. Linear regression models examined whether baseline social media use and average annual change predicted psychological distress (K6) and mental well-being (SWEMWBS) at age 23 among 6,123 participants (54% female; age = 23 years), adjusting for prior psychological distress, mental well-being, and sociodemographic factors.
Results
Social media use followed an inverted-U trajectory, increasing during adolescence
 


before declining into early adulthood. Higher baseline social media use and a greater rate of increase were independently associated with higher psychological distress and lower mental well-being at age 23 after adjustment for prior psychological distress, mental well-being, and sociodemographic factors. Gender-stratified analyses indicated that these associations were more pronounced among females. Among males, social media trajectory components showed little evidence of association with either outcome after adjustment.
Conclusions
Developmental patterns of social media use from adolescence to early adulthood are associated with psychological distress and mental well-being in early adulth

title: Computing Cohen’s dz from commonly reported statistics: a practical guide for the meta-analysis of paired samples mean differences
description: The typical approach to computing standardised mean differences (d) from paired-samples (or within-subjects) designs requires knowing the often-unreported repeated-measures correlation, r_"repeated" . This adjustment enables comparison with ds from independent samples. However, when meta-analysing effects that come exclusively from paired samples, an underutilised option is to compute Cohen’s d_z, which does not require r_"repeated" . Because d_z can be computed from a range of summary and inferential statistics, it enables researchers to obtain standardised effects even when articles report their results in little detail. The present article contains equations and the corresponding R code needed to compute d_z and its variance, and adjust for small-sample bias.

title: People Regulate Emotions During Spontaneous Thought Using Detectable Linguistic Signatures
description: Emotion regulation is a central process through which people shape their emotional lives, yet much of what we know comes from studies of deliberate attempts at regulation. In everyday life, regulation may emerge spontaneously, embedded within the flow of ongoing thought. If so, patterns of spontaneous thought–both what people think about and how thoughts move between ideas–may provide a window into emotion regulation as it naturally unfolds. Across two studies, we used a Think-Aloud paradigm to capture participants’ unconstrained streams of thought and tested whether these patterns captured strategy use and predicted mental health. In Study 1, participants (N = 233) completed nearly 5,000 Think-Aloud sessions over three weeks. We trained logistic regression models to identify emotion regulation strategy signatures and tested their generalizability on held-out data. We then tested whether expression of these signatures predicted emotional outcomes in held-out data from Study 1 and in an independent sample from Study 2 (N = 780). Models identified six strategies–acceptance, rumination, savoring, expressive amplification, reappraisal, and distraction. The strongest features aligned with theory (e.g., rumination with negative, repetitive thoughts; reappraisal with self-referential content and fewer positive-to-negative transitions). Moreover, individual differences in strategy expression predicted momentary changes in valence and both state and trait anxiety: acceptance, reappraisal, and savoring were associated with greater emotional improvement and lower anxiety, whereas rumination showed the opposite pattern. These findings reveal how emotion regulation is reflected in the content and dynamics of spontaneous thought and provide a predictive tool for identifying regulatory processes in everyday thinking.

title: Putting Journal-based Peer Review in Its Place: Transparency, Post-publication Review, and Systems for Self-correction
description:
Journal-based peer review is a widely used form of gatekeeping in science, and publication in a peer-reviewed journal is often treated as a reliable indicator of credible research. In this paper, we argue that this view of journal-based peer review is misguided. Currently, journal-based peer review rarely prioritises checking accuracy, and journals typically do not require submitted research to be reported transparently enough to do so. Moreover, vetting research requires much more time and expertise than can be feasibly achieved by an editor and a small number of peer-reviewers. With more investment in accuracy checking, journal-based peer review could serve as a reliable initial quality filter. However, comprehensive quality vetting requires an expanded pool of experts, which, we argue, can only be achieved via post-publication review.
At present, there is little infrastructure to support post-publication review and few incentives for researchers to engage in it. We propose a fully transparent system in which all manuscripts submitted for publication are posted to a central repository alongside editorial decisions, peer review reports, data, code, and as they become available, post-publication reviews. Post-publication review efforts could be directed toward the most promising, influential, or high-stakes research. Evidence syntheses, such as meta-analyses, offer a natural focal point for post-publication review, provided they prioritise rigorous assessment of the primary literature.

title: The effects of introducing measurement-based care in outpatient psychotherapy: a naturalistic study
description:
Objective. This study investigated the effectiveness of a newly implemented routine outcome
monitoring and feedback system, the Greifswald Psychotherapy Navigation System (GPNS).
Methods. 261 patients (M_age = 33 years, 79% female) completed individual cognitive-
behavioral therapy (CBT) from 61 psychotherapists. Using Bayesian instrumental variable
estimation, we estimated the local average treatment effect (LATE) of using the GPNS on
post-treatment symptom severity and personality impairment. Sensitivity analyses were
performed to estimate the robustness of our estimates.
Results. Using the GPNS significantly reduced post-treatment symptom severity (LATE = -
0.11, 95% CrI: -0.18; -0.03) and personality impairment (LATE = -2.65, 95% CrI: -3.72; -
1.58). Sensitivity analyses showed that the effects were robust to plausible residual
confounding.
Conclusion. The GPNS shows promise for improving outcomes of CBT. Future prospective
trials should be conducted to obtain a more generalizable estimate of its effect.

title: The Psychological Scaffolding of Number
description: Theories of numeracy assume that logical concepts which enable formal definitions of numbers (cardinality, one-to-one correspondence, successor function) play key roles in ontogenesis, but empirical support is lacking. These concepts may not be suitable targets for theory because such definitions are not explanatory of what numbers are. We propose a correspondence between formal ideas that are generative for the natural numbers and children’s cognitive representations prior to numeracy (‘precursor system’). Building on our account of arithmetic foundations (Grice et al., 2024), we give a new construction of the natural numbers – the first to not rely on the Peano axioms – and corresponding theory of numeracy. We show that numbers can be derived from linear order, monotonicity and convexity – evolved principles of perceptual organization. A key concept is the ℕ-chain, the limit of finite linear orders, which we show is equivalent to the successor function but can be approximated in finite terms. The precursor system is based on linear orders – number word sequences, play schemas – and their relationships in the count routine by order embedding (structure mapping). It develops gradually, with order isomorphisms as proto-cardinality, reaching a representation analogous to an ℕ-chain when the child realizes that numbers never end. Ordinality precedes cardinality, one-to-one correspondence results from minimal (and therefore convex) embeddings, and numbers and arithmetic emerge as a common structure. Our theory shows that numeracy is both culturally-mediated and biologically-based, although ‘core number systems’ do not play a direct role. The dawning of numeracy in a child’s mind is both a reinvention and rediscovery of mathematics itself.
